coredata.dc:title: NiCo layered double hydroxides/carbon dots composites with S-doing for high-performance asymmetric supercapacitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xin
Sun, Jiale
Zhang, Lin
Li, Zhongjun
Yao, Hongchang
Liu, Qingchao
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: The pursuit of high-performance electrode materials for supercapacitors has led to the exploration of transition metal layered double hydroxides (LDH), which boast an impressive theoretical specific capacitance. However, their intrinsic limitations, such as poor electronic conductivity and slow diffusion kinetics, have posed significant challenges for their practical implementation. In this work, a NiCo LDH/carbon dots (CDs) composite with S-doping has been meticulously designed to overcome the aforementioned limitations through a synergistic combination of heteroatom doping and carbon incorporation. Utilizing a one-pot hydrothermal method, the composite electrode for the application of asymmetric supercapacitors have been in-situ growth on Ni foam substrate, which not only serves as a robust support but also contributes to the formation of NiCo LDH by providing a uniform Ni source, thereby ensuring strong adhesion and structural integrity. The morphology and microstructures of composites could be finely modulated by adjusting CDs amount. With moderate CDs contents, the optimum electrode S-LDH/CDs-2 exhibits distinctive 3D needle-like spherical morphology, coupled with the ample space for ion diffusion. Consequently, the electrode demonstrates outstanding electrochemical performance, featuring a high areal capacitance of 5157 mF cm−2 at a current density of 2 mA cm−2. Additionally, it exhibits noteworthy rate performance and maintains excellent cycling stability. Integrating the innovative electrode into asymmetric supercapacitors configuration, superior energy density (0.24–0.45 mWh cm−2) and power density (4–20 mW cm−2) as well as good stability have been achieved for the S-LDH/CDs-2//AC device. These results indicate that S-doped NiCo LDH/CDs composite electrode has the desirable potential to be a candidate for energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Layered double hydroxide
Sulfur doping
Supercapacitors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the S-doped composites of carbon dots and NiCo layered double hydroxides
Assembly of an asymmetric supercapacitor device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of CoCH, S-LDH and S-LDH/CDs-2.
(a) TEM, (b) HRTEM, (c) EDS images of S-LDH/CDs-2.
(a) Full XPS, and high-resolution spectra for S-LDH/CDs-2, (b) Ni 2p, (c) Co 2p, (d) O 1 s, (e) S 2p, (f) C 1 s, (g) N 1 s.
SEM images of (a,b) S-LDH, (c,d) S-LDH/CDs-1, (e,f) S-LDH/CDs-2 and (g,h) S-LDH/CDs-3.

Electrochemical performances for the electrodes (All the test were repeated for three times): (a) CV curves at 5 mV s−1, (b) the plots of log (v) versus log (i) of the S-LDH/CDs-2, (c) GCD curves at 2 mA cm−2, (d) specific capacitances at 2–20 mA cm−2, (e) Nyquist plots of EIS and equivalent circuit mode (inset), (f) cycling performance of S-LDH and S-LDH/CDs-2 at a current density of 30 mA cm−2.

Electrochemical performances for the S-LDH/CDs-2//AC asymmetric supercapacitor device (All the test were repeated for three times): (a) CV curves in different potential windows at 10 mV s−1, (b) the charge and discharge curves at different current densities from 5 to 50 mA cm−2, (c) Ragone plot, (d) cycle performance at a current density of 50 mA cm−2 (Inset: a bright LED indicator and an mini motor fan powered by the fully charged devices.)
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedures of CoCH, S-LDH, and S-LDH/CD-2 composite.
The equivalent circuit fitting parameters for various samples.

head.ce:title: NiCo layered double hydroxides/carbon dots composites with S-doing for high-performance asymmetric supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pursuit of high-performance electrode materials for supercapacitors has led to the exploration of transition metal layered double hydroxides (LDH), which boast an impressive theoretical specific capacitance. However, their intrinsic limitations, such as poor electronic conductivity and slow diffusion kinetics, have posed significant challenges for their practical implementation. In this work, a NiCo LDH/carbon dots (CDs) composite with S-doping has been meticulously designed to overcome the aforementioned limitations through a synergistic combination of heteroatom doping and carbon incorporation. Utilizing a one-pot hydrothermal method, the composite electrode for the application of asymmetric supercapacitors have been in-situ growth on Ni foam substrate, which not only serves as a robust support but also contributes to the formation of NiCo LDH by providing a uniform Ni source, thereby ensuring strong adhesion and structural integrity. The morphology and microstructures of composites could be finely modulated by adjusting CDs amount. With moderate CDs contents, the optimum electrode S-LDH/CDs-2 exhibits distinctive 3D needle-like spherical morphology, coupled with the ample space for ion diffusion. Consequently, the electrode demonstrates outstanding electrochemical performance, featuring a high areal capacitance of 5157 mF cm−2 at a current density of 2 mA cm−2. Additionally, it exhibits noteworthy rate performance and maintains excellent cycling stability. Integrating the innovative electrode into asymmetric supercapacitors configuration, superior energy density (0.24–0.45 mWh cm−2) and power density (4–20 mW cm−2) as well as good stability have been achieved for the S-LDH/CDs-2//AC device. These results indicate that S-doped NiCo LDH/CDs composite electrode has the desirable potential to be a candidate for energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Design and development of efficient energy storage systems such as batteries, fuel cells and supercapacitors for application of electric vehicles and portable electronic devices in modern society has become imperative due to the limited fossil fuels and pressing issues of environment pollution and climate change [1,2]. Supercapacitors are particularly attractive because they have much higher power density, longer cycle life, and stronger safety performance compared with batteries. Unfortunately, supercapacitors have lower energy density than batteries [3,4]. To circumvent this constraint, novel electrode materials with abundant reversible redox sites and good electric conductivity, as well as system design such as asymmetric supercapacitors have been investigated intensively in recent years.

Binary metal layered double hydroxides (LDH) are esteemed for their adjustable composition, outstanding theoretical specific capacity, and diverse oxidation state combinations, positioning them as prime contenders for high-performance asymmetric supercapacitors positive electrodes. However, their intrinsic electronic conductivity is relatively low, impeding the rapid storage and release of electrons and, consequently, detracting from their capacitance performance [5–7]. To address this limitation, the incorporation of conductive materials with LDH has emerged as an effective strategy. This approach not only bolsters the overall conductivity but also enhances the utilization of the active ingredients within the LDH. Carbon materials such as graphene [8], carbon nanotube (CNT) [9] and etc. are outstanding candidates because of their excellent electrical conductivity, chemical stability and rich resources. Carbon dots (CDs) as a zero-dimensional carbon material have natural merits for constructing various structures than the multidimensional carbon materials [10,11]. Various nanocomposites like CDs/NiFe Prussian blue analogues (PBAs) [12], CDs/Co-Ni(OH)2 [13], CDs/NiCo-LDH powder [14], CDs/CoP [15] have shown great potential as excellent electrode materials.

Building upon this composite strategy, doping has also been extensively employed in materials science to further refine their performance. The metal doping (Mn, Al, Fe, Mo, Ga, etc.) has been testified to adjust composition and interfacial activity of LDH [16–20]. Recently, more and more non-metal doped LDH have been investigated such as Se, S and F. For example, Luo et al. [21] obtained NiCoFe-LDH-Se/NiCo-O-Se composites and found that the doping nonmetallic element Se can effectively modulate electronic structure and expose multiple redox active sites. Hao et al. [22] constructed S-Co(OH)2@graphene composite electrode and demonstrated that S can tune the electronic environment around Co to enhance intrinsic conductivity of Co(OH)2. So far, S-doped LDH such as NiCo and NiFe LDH have predominantly been explored within the realm of catalysis [23,24]. There is an urgent need for more in-depth and methodical investigation into their use in energy storage applications. Moreover, the simplicity of the doping operation, when synergized with the conductive properties of carbon composites, would be anticipated to offer a dual-pronged approach to optimize the electrochemical properties of LDHs.

On these context, in this work S-doped composites of NiCo LDH and CDs with 3D flower-like morphology on Ni foam have been prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method and used as the self-standing positive electrode of asymmetric supercapacitors. The doped S2− ions can boost surface reaction feature of the composite electrode by increasing the energy barrier for the intercalation of OH− ions into the interlayer space of NiCo LDH, and thus improving the rate performance of electrode under high current density charging and discharging. The morphology of composite could be tuned by controlling the dosage of CDs since it acts as nucleation sites guiding the directional growth of LDH crystals. The optimum S-LDH/CDs-2 composite electrode presents decreased impedances, high areal specific capacitance, outstanding rate performance, and excellent cycle stability under the cooperation of CDs and S-doing. The assembled supercapacitor achieved a high energy density and power density.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, ammonium fluoride, urea, sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate, hydrogen peroxide solution (30 %) from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The deionized water was obtained from the laboratory. Nickel foam (99.8 % purity) and activated carbon were supplied by Xinxiang Heluelida Power Materials Co., Ltd. Ni foam was cut into pieces (1 × 1 cm2) and ultrasonicated in acetone, 3 M hydrochloric acid and deionized water for 10 min sequentially [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the S-doped composites of carbon dots and NiCo layered double hydroxides
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped CDs was obtained by hydrothermal treatment of the mixed aqueous solution (15 mL) of citric acid (1.0 M) and urea (1.2 M) at 180 °C for 5 h [18]. For preparation of CDs and NiCo layered double hydroxides composites with S-doping, a certain amount of CDs were added into a mixture of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.29 g), urea (0.36 g), NH4F (0.089 g), Na2S2O3·5H2O (0.05 g) in 20 mL deionized water and stirred for 20 min. To the above mixture was added 200 μL of H2O2 (30 %) with stirring for 10 min, and transferred to a Teflon-line autoclave. Six pieces of Ni foam were placed into the autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 6 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, washed with deionized water, and dried at 60 °C overnight. The as-synthesized samples are designated as S-LDH, S-LDH/CDs-1 and S-LDH/CDs-2, S-LDH/CDs-3 for the CDs amount of 0, 2.5, 5 and 10 mg, respectively. For comparison, CoCH was synthesized in a similar procedure as that of S-LDH, except for no addition of Na2S2O3·5H2O and H2O2.

The morphology and nanostructure of the materials were measured with the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TM-1000) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM2100F). Chemical bonds were detected by FT-IR spectrum (Nicolet-Nexus 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer). The product powders were scraped off from the Ni Foam substrate and examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, X'PertPRO diffractometer) with Ka radiation (1.5418 Å) for analysis of the crystalline phases. The surface chemical states of the samples were detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). The Ni, Co, S contents were tested by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES, Thermo Fisher 6300).

The electrochemical performances of the electrodes were tested with a three-electrode configuration, in which the as-synthesized sample was directly used as the working electrodes, the saturated calomel electrode and Pt plate (1 × 1 cm2) adopted as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. 1 M Na2SO4 and 3 M KOH were used as electrolyte in the study, respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were tested by CHI 660 electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China) and the galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) curves were obtained by Land test system (LANHE, Wuhan, China). The areal specific capacity of electrode, C
s (F cm−2), was calculated from the galvanostatic discharge curves according to the Eq. (1).
(1)
C s
=
I × t
/
Δ V × S

where I, t, ΔV and S represents the current (A), the discharge time (s) and voltage window (V), electrode area (cm2), respectively. C
s was also calculated from CV integrated areas by the Eq. (2)
(2)
C s
=
A
2 SkΔV
where A is the area inside the CV curve (AV), k is the scan rate (V s−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of an asymmetric supercapacitor device
ce:section.ce:para:
The asymmetric supercapacitor was assembled with S-LDH/CDs −2 as the positive and activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode with 3 M KOH as electrolyte. AC negative electrode was prepared by pressing a mixture of the AC (80 wt%), acetylene black (10 wt%), and polytetrafluoroethylene binder (10 wt%) onto a Ni foam grid with an area of 1 cm2 . The mass ratio of negative and positive electrode was about 2 in order to balance the charge of positive and negative electrodes. The energy density (E, Wh cm−2) and power density (P, W cm−2) were determined based on the following Eqs. (3) and (4):
(3)
E =
C s
× ∆
V 2
/ 7200
(4)
P = E × 3600 / Δ t

The synthetic pathway of CoCH, S-LDH and S-LDH/CDs is explicitly presented in Scheme 1 . The CoCH nanoneedles are generated in situ on the Ni foam by Co2+ combining with the hydroxide and carbonate from thermally decomposed urea, demonstrated by XRD spectrum in Fig. 1 . Differently, S-LDH exhibits typical diffraction peaks at 11.7°, 23.1°, 34.8°, 39.5°, 60.8° indexed to (003), (006), (012), (015) and (110) planes of NiCo LDH (JCPDS card No.40-0216), which was formed via following possible reactions [26,27]. Ni foam was etched by H+ produced by hydrolysis of Na2S2O3 to supply Ni2+ (formulas (5)–(6)). M2+ ions (M represents Ni/Co) and partial oxidized Co3+ ions by O2 combine with OH− coming from hydrolysis of urea and thus the NiCo hydroxides crystal nuclei in situ formed onto the nickel foam substrate (formulas (7)–(10)). Simultaneously, by help of H2O2 the hydrolysis of S2O3 2− provides abundant S2− in alkaline environment to make S-LDH (formula (11)).
(5)
2S 2
O 3
2 −
+
2H 2
O 2
→
O 2
+
2SO 4
2 −
+
2H 2
S
(6)
H 2
S →
2H +
+
S
2 −
(7)
C O
NH 2
2
+
2H 2
O →
H 2
CO 3
+
2NH 3
(8)
NH 3
+
H 2
O →
NH 4
+
+
OH −
(9)
2Co
NH 3
6
2 +
+
H 2
O + 1 / 2
O 2
→ 2Co
NH 3
6
3 +
+
2OH −
(10)
M
2 +
+
2OH −
→ M
OH
2
(11)
M
OH
2
+ x
S
2 −
→
S x
− M
OH
2 - 2 x
+ 2
xOH -

When CDs are incorporated, they coat on the newly formed hydroxides crystal nuclei due to the coordination effects, which will control crystal growth and thus regulate the morphology and microstructure. Determined by Scherrer's equation [28],
(12)
D =
Kλ βcosθ
where D is the average crystallite size, β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM), λ is the X-ray wavelength, and k is the form factor (0.9), the average crystallite size for CoCH, S-LDH, S-LDH/CDs-1, S-LDH/CDs-2, and S-LDH/CDs-3 are of 32, 32, 45, 46 and 37 nm, respectively. The size of the grains increases with the presence of carbon dots. In addition, CDs can boost the electrical conductivity of the composites and stabilize surface state, and thus enhancing the specific capacitance and stability.

To confirm the successful S doping and combination of NiCo LDH with CDs, the representative TEM images, XPS and ICP-AES spectra of S-LDH/CDs-2 composite are detected. The coating of CDs with a size of about 5 nm on LDH ultrathin nanoneedles is observed in Fig. 2a. The clear lattice fringes of 0.37 nm corresponds to (006) plane of LDH and 0.27 nm relates to CDs in HRTEM image (Fig. 2b) [29]. The coexistence of Ni, Co, O, S, C and N elements and their uniform distribution are revealed by the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping in Fig. 2c. Note that sulfur doping is uniformly distributed throughout the entire composite material. This is because the composite is prepared through a one-pot reaction involving a sulfur source and carbon dots. These carbon dots are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, which makes them particularly receptive to sulfur doping. The ICP-AES test shows 1.76:1 of Co/Ni molar ratio, and 0.63 % of S content in S-LDH/CDs-2 (Table. S1) revealing the comparatively low content of S atoms. The survey XPS spectrum of S-LDH/CDs-2 identifies the surface elemental composition of Ni, Co, O, S, C and N and electronic states in greater detail (Fig. 3a). In the Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 3b), the fitting peaks at 855.8 eV (Ni 2p3/2) and 873.4 eV (Ni 2p1/2) correspond to Ni2+, and the fitting peaks at 858.0 eV (Ni 2p3/2) and 875.8 eV (Ni 2p1/2) correspond to Ni3+, the other fitting peaks are satellite peaks [14]. In the Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 3c), the fitting peak at 781.0 eV (Co 2p3/2) corresponds to Co3+, the fitting peak at 783.5 eV (Co 2p3/2) corresponds to Co2+, the peak at 797.1 corresponds to Co 2p1/2, and the remaining peaks are satellite peaks [14,30]. This XPS result conforms the existence of trivalent metal ions in S-LDH/CDs-2, which can stabilize the a-phase hydroxide and improving the electrochemical properties. The fitting O 1 s peaks at 529.6 eV, 531.3 eV, and 532.6 eV were attributed to Ni-O&Co-O, OH, and interlayer adsorbed water, respectively (Fig. 3d) [31]. S-doping can be corroborated by S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e), the fitting peaks at the 162.1 eV and 165.0 eV positions correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, and the peak at the 168.7 eV is attributed to the SO bond [32,33]. The spectral diagram of C1s (Fig. 3f) shows fitting peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.6 eV and 289.3 eV positions, corresponding to C-C/C=C, CN and C-C=O, respectively [34]. N-doping of CDs can be testified by the two N 1 s peaks in Fig. 3g located at 399.7 eV and 403.2 eV, correspond to pyrrole nitrogen and graphitized nitrogen, respectively [31].

The effect of CDs content on the structure and morphology of CDs/LDH are investigated. As shown in Fig. S1a, all S-LDH/CDs composites with various amount of CDs display identical XRD diffraction peaks compared with S-LDH, which suggests that the introduction of CDs has no effect on the crystal structure of NiCo LDH. FTIR spectra (Fig. S1b) suggest that all S-CDs/LDH composites exhibit peaks of S-LDH located at approximately 3450 cm−1 (OH), 1636 cm−1(H2O), 1386 and 833 cm−1 (NO3 −) [35], 1000–470 cm−1 region (M-O and M-O-M on the laminate) [36], as well as characteristic peaks of CDs at 2933 and 2867 cm−1 corresponding to the typical CH stretching vibrations [37]. The morphology evolutions of S-LDH/CDs with various contents of CDs are shown in Fig. 4 . For the pure S-LDH, flowers-like spheres grown on Ni foam surface are observed, which are composed of lance-shaped nanoflakes petals. When CDs were incorporated, the obtained S-LDH/CDs-1 has kept spherical morphology but with nanoneedle-like petals, and note that plenty of nanoparticles are distributed uniformly on them. As more CDs (5 mg) were added during synthesis, the nanoparticles disappear and the surfaces of nanoneedles become smooth. When amount of CDs further increased to 10 mg, the S-LDH/CDs-3 appears as spheres formed by densely interlaced nanosheets. We speculate the formation mechanism for the different morphologies of the products as follows. The formation 2D bimetallic (Ni, Co) hydroxides nanoflakes in S-LDH is caused by the difference of electronic structure between Co2+ (octahedral coordination) and Ni2+ (square-planar coordination) [31]. However, when CDs are incorporated in the reaction, they combine metal ions by coordination reactions. Free Ni ions in the solution are greatly reduced because of their lower initial concentration than Co ions, which would slacken the growth rate of bimetallic hydroxides. The rest of free Co ions in the solution are preferential to produce needle-like hydroxides, which can be demonstrated by SEM image of CoCH (Fig. S2). Then CDs deposit on the nanoneedles' surfaces, with them as cores the bimetallic hydroxides gradually grow into nanocrystal particles. The integral coating of CDs facilitates the smooth surfaces. When most metal ions in the solution coordinated with excess CDs, bimetallic hydroxides nanograins preferentially in-situ grow on CDs which haphazardly deposited on the substrate, and finally leads to densely interlaced nanosheets.

The capacitive performances of S-LDH/CDs composites electrodes accompanied with S-LDH and CoCH were evaluated by a range of electrochemical techniques including CV, GCD and EIS measurements in three-electrode system. Two electrolytes of Na2SO4 and KOH were employed in the study. In 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte, S-LDH/CDs-2 has specific capacitance of 2242 mF cm−2 at current density of 2 mA cm−2, which is calculated from the charging/discharging curves in the potential window of 0.38 V (Fig. S3a). CV curves at scan rates of 5, 10, and 20 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. S3b, where the CV shapes almost remained constant, indicating a good rate performance. The calculated specific capacitances are 836, 359, 153 mF cm−2, respectively. Furthermore, after 3000 cycles charging and discharging at the current density of 30 mA cm−2, 79 % initial capacitance and 100 % Coulombic efficiency can be maintained (Fig. S3c). In contrast, the KOH electrolyte demonstrates superior capacitance performances compared to the Na2SO4 solution. Fig. 5a presents CV curves for CoCH, S-LDH, S-LDH/CDs-1, S-LDH/CDs-2, and S-LDH/CDs-3 in KOH within a voltage window of −0.1–0.5 V at the scan rate of 5 mV s−1. S-LDH and S-LDH/CDs composites electrodes show enhanced redox waves than that of CoCH, attributing to the conversion of Ni(II)/Ni(III), Co(II)/Co(III) and Co(III)/Co(IV) [33]. In order to further understand the storage mechanism of composite materials, a semi-quantitative analysis of CV curves of S-LDH/CDs-2 composite electrode at various scan rates was carried out. Fig. 5b provides the relationship between the logarithm of redox peak current density (i) and logarithm of scan rates (v), which satisfies the following equation where a is a constant b is the slope value of the plot:
log i = log a + b log v (13).

When b approaches 1, the redox reaction is surface controlled process; while b is 0.5, it is diffusion controlled process [38]. The b values for S-LDH/CDs-2 are around 0.80, indicating the mixed diffusion and surface control process, which is caused by synergistic effect of CDs and S-doping. The doped S2− ions can increase the energy barrier for the intercalation of OH− ions into the interlayer space of NiCo LDH, and thus boosting surface reaction feature of the composite electrode. Combing capacitive feature of CDs, the obtained composite electrode has both battery-like and supercapacitor-like characteristics [14]. Fig. 5c shows the galvanostatic discharge curves at 2 mA cm−2. The discharge time of S-LDH/CDs-2 is much longer than that of CoCH, S-LDH and other S-LDH/CDs composite samples, demonstrating the greatly enhanced charge storage capability. Fig. 5d gives the areal specific capacitances calculated from GCD curves at different current densities. For S-LDH/CDs-2, the areal specific capacitances are 5157, 5102, 4816, 4351, 3805 and 3135 mF cm−2 at current density of 2, 4, 6, 10, 16 and 20 mA cm−2, respectively, suggesting excellent rate performance of the composite. The EIS Nyquist plots of these samples are shown in Fig. 5e and the corresponding fitting parameters with chi-squared less than 10−3 are listed in Table 1 , using the equivalent circuit in the inset of Fig. 5e. The error rates of Warburg impedance values in the fitting process for CoCH, S-LDH, S-LDH/CDs-1, S-LDH/CDs-2, and S-LDH/CDs-3 are 19 %, 3 %, 5.5 %, 2.7 % and 1.9 %, respectively. The equivalent series resistance (R
s ) and charge transfer resistance (R ct) value of S-LDH/CDs-2 is 0.80 Ω and 0.19 Ω, respectively, much lower than other materials. The AC conductivity (σ, S cm−1) was calculated using the Eq. (14):
(14)
σ =
d
R
c t
A
where A is the area of the electrode and d is the thickness. The conductivity for CoCH, S-LDH, S-LDH/CDs-1, S-LDH/CDs-2, and S-LDH/CDs-3 are 0.11, 0.36, 0.32, 0.4, and 0.36 S cm−1, respectively. These results indicate that the incorporation of CDs and S-doping significantly enhance the intrinsic electrical conductivity of the composite materials. In addition, the higher slope at lower frequency for the S-LDH/CDs-2 indicates its better conductivity of the charges within the electroactive materials [39]. The greatly enhanced capacitive properties of S-LDH/CDs-2 are ascribed on the contribution of improved conductivity and the unique 3D needle-like spherical structure with more exposed active sites and adequate space for ion diffusion caused by structural regulation of CDs and S-doping. Additionally, EIS measurements were performed at various temperatures, specifically at 10, 20, 40, and 60 °C, with the results depicted in Fig. S4. The analysis of the EIS curves reveals a positive correlation between temperature and electrical conductivity. The cycling stabilities of S-LDH/CDs-2 and S-LDH electrodes were evaluated via charge/discharge at the current density of 30 mA cm−2. As shown in Fig. 5f, the capacitance retention of S-LDH/CDs-2 electrode (76 %) is superior to that of S-LDH (62 %) after 3000 cycles. The improvement of the stability for the S-LDH/CDs-2 electrode is due to the fact that CDs can provide the protection skeleton to alleviate structural collapse during charge and discharge processes. The cyclability of the S-LDH/CDs-2 composite was also evaluated by CV (at scan rate of 20 mV s−1), as illustrated in Fig. S5. The current response shape and the peak potential position changed very little, the calculated capacitance from CV area at 10000th cycle is 83 % of that at 1th cycle. Fig. S6a shows the XRD patterns for S-LDH/CDs-2 electrode before and after 10,000 cycles. After cycles, the electrode displays diffraction peaks corresponding to NiOOH (at 12.9°and 25.8°), CoS and NiS (29.8°), CoS2 (32.4°) and NiS2 (39.1°). The peaks belonging to NiCo LDH have disappeared compared with those of electrode before cycles. In addition, the strong nickel diffraction peak observed after cycling is due to the XRD analysis being conducted directly on the NF electrode loaded with active material. While, the sample before cycling was prepared by scraping off the active material from the NF for testing. A comparative analysis of optical photographs taken before and after the electrode cycling process demonstrates that the composite material maintains its structural integrity after undergoing repeated charge and discharge cycles (Fig. S6b). Fig. S6(c-d) shows SEM images for S-LDH/CDs-2 electrode after 10000th CV measurement. It can be seen that the Ni foam substrate is uniformly covered with solid nanoparticles that exhibit porosity, and flower-like nanosheets are located upon it. Compared to the morphology before the cyclic experiments (Fig. 4e-f), the original nano-needle structure has vanished. Drawing from these observations, the observed decrease in capacitance retention can primarily be attributed to some irreversible reactions occurring within the NiCo hydroxide active material during the ultra-high current (30 mA) charging and discharging processes, which lead to alterations in the material's morphology and phase.

The asymmetric supercapacitor of S-LDH/CDs-2//AC was assembled by using S-LDH/CDs-2 as positive electrode, AC as negative electrode and 3 M KOH as electrolyte for practical utilization. The working voltage can be broaden to 1.6 V, further increase to 1.8 V an intensive anodic peak appears due to the water splitting, and thus 0–1.6 V is applied to subsequent tests (Fig. 6a). CV curves of the asymmetric supercapacitor at scan rates of 10, 20, 30, 50, and 100 mV s−1 were conducted in Fig. S7, and the specific capacitances calculated from the integrated areas according to Eq. (2) are 886, 696, 560, 418, 268 mF cm−2, respectively. The linear GCD curves of this device at different current density in Fig. 6b exhibit typical characterization of supercapacitor. The specific capacities are calculated to be 1278 and 892 mF cm−2 at 5 and 15 mA cm−2, respectively, suggesting prominent rate performance. The Ragone plot of the energy density and the power density was shown in Fig. 6c. The device delivers an energy density of 0.45 mWh cm−2 at a power density of 4 mW cm−2, and still retains an energy density of 0.24 mWh cm−2 at a high-power density of 20 mW cm−2. Such performance are highly competitive with the recent reports for Ni/Co-based asymmetrical supercapacitor, such as Ni3Co1 LDH@G//AC (0.27 mWh cm−2 at 0.9 mW cm−2) [40], ZnO@NiCo-LDH //AC (0.044 mWh cm−2 at 1.17 mW cm−2) [41], Co(OH)2/CoOOH/Co3O4/Cu(OH)2//CC (0.196 mWh cm−2 at 1.6 mW cm−2) [42], Cu(OH)2/Co(OH)2//AC (0.035 mWh cm−2 at 1.87 mW cm−2) [43], Ni(OH)2//AC(0.148 mWh cm−2 at 1.4 mW cm−2) [44], CNT@Ni3V2O8@NiCo2S4//AC (0.414 mWh cm−2 at 0.8 mW cm−2) [45]. A more detailed comparison on the performances is shown in Table S2. Fig. 6 d shows the long-term lifespan of this device with a capacitance retention of approximately 85 % after 5000 cycles. Moreover, its practical applications such as powering motor fans and lighting LED lights are displayed in the inset in Fig. 6d.

In summary, S-doped NiCo LDH/CDs composites with adjustable morphologies were constructed on nickel foam by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, where nickel foam specially acts as both conductive substrates and nickel sources for uniform growth of NiCo LDH. The morphologies of as-prepared composites are turned from nanoneedle-like to nanosheet-like with increase of the amount of CDs. The optimal S-LDH/CDs-2 exhibits largest areal specific capacitance of 5157 mF cm−2 at 2 mA cm−2, superior rate performance and significantly enhanced stability attributed to the unique porous structure with more exposed active sites and adequate space for ion diffusion and improved conductivity provided by CDs skeleton and S-doping. The as-assembled S-LDH/CDs-2//AC supercapacitor achieves satisfying energy and power densities at the broaden voltage, as well as long cycling life. The structure modulation of CDs and synergistic effect with sulfur doping in this work will bring a promising way to push the development of advanced composite materials for energy storage.

Xin Zhu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Jiale Sun: Investigation. Lin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Zhongjun Li: Project administration. Hongchang Yao: Supervision. Qingchao Liu: Resources. Zhaohui Li: Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974125).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111514.
